title: Reproduction
summary: Reproduction is how life continues, and D3.1 asks a sharper question than 'how are offspring made': how does the type of reproduction shape the genetic variation that evolution then acts on. This lesson builds the whole picture — sexual versus asexual reproduction and their very different consequences for variation; gametes, fertilisation and the way meiosis produces haploid gametes while fertilisation restores the diploid number; an overview of reproduction in flowering plants (pollination, fertilisation, seed and fruit); and reproduction in humans, including the roles of the male and female gametes and the hormonal control of the menstrual cycle by FSH, LH, oestrogen and progesterone through negative and positive feedback. Every worked example and the model answer are laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks, so you can see how each distinct idea earns its credit.
learningObjectives: Compare sexual and asexual reproduction and explain their contrasting consequences for genetic variation in offspring.
Describe gametes and fertilisation, and explain how meiosis produces haploid gametes while fertilisation restores the diploid number.
Outline reproduction in flowering plants, including pollination, fertilisation and the formation of seed and fruit.

Outline reproduction in humans, including the roles of the male and female gametes and the hormonal control of the menstrual cycle by FSH, LH, oestrogen and progesterone through negative and positive feedback.
Present answers so that each distinct comparative or explanatory point earns its analytic mark, with error-carried-forward protecting the rest.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Two Ways to Make the Next Generation
simpleExplanation.summary: Living things reproduce in one of two broad ways. Asexual reproduction copies one parent, so the offspring are genetic clones. Sexual reproduction mixes genetic material from two parents through gametes, so the offspring are genetically different from each other and from their parents. That difference in variation is the heart of the topic.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of asexual reproduction as photocopying a document: every copy is identical to the original, it is fast, and you only need one machine. Sexual reproduction is more like taking one page from each of two different documents and shuffling them together to write a brand-new page each time. It is slower and needs two sources, but no two results are the same — and that constant novelty is exactly what lets a population cope when conditions change.
steps: Asexual reproduction involves one parent and (usually) mitosis, so offspring are genetically identical clones — fast and reliable when the environment is stable.
Sexual reproduction involves two parents. Meiosis makes haploid gametes that carry half the chromosome number, and each gamete is genetically unique.
At fertilisation two gametes fuse, restoring the full diploid chromosome number and combining alleles from both parents.
The result is offspring that vary genetically, giving the population the raw material for adaptation and natural selection.

flashcards.back: Reproduction involving a single parent and no fusion of gametes. Offspring are produced by mitosis and are genetically identical to the parent (clones). Fast and requires no mate, but produces very little genetic variation.

Reproduction involving the fusion of two haploid gametes, usually from two parents, to form a diploid zygote. Offspring are genetically different from one another and from their parents, generating genetic variation.
A specialised HAPLOID sex cell (sperm/pollen and egg/ovule) produced by meiosis. Carries one set of chromosomes so that two gametes fusing restore the diploid number.
The fusion of a male and a female gamete to form a diploid zygote. It restores the full chromosome number (2n) and combines alleles from two parents, adding to genetic variation.

Meiosis halves the chromosome number to make HAPLOID (n) gametes. Without it, fertilisation would double the chromosome number every generation. Meiosis (n) then fertilisation (back to 2n) keeps the number constant.

flashcards.front: Two sources of variation in sexual reproduction
flashcards.back: 1) Meiosis shuffles alleles (independent assortment and crossing over) so every gamete is unique. 2) Fertilisation randomly combines two different gametes. Together they make each offspring genetically distinct.

Offspring are genetically IDENTICAL clones of the parent, so asexual reproduction produces essentially no genetic variation (only rare mutation). This is why a clonal population is vulnerable to a new disease or environmental change.
The transfer of pollen (containing the male gamete) from the anther to the stigma of a flower, by wind or animals. It precedes fertilisation and is NOT the same event as fertilisation.
After pollination, a pollen tube grows down the style to the ovule, and the male gamete fuses with the female gamete (egg cell) inside the ovule to form a diploid zygote.
After fertilisation the ovule develops into a SEED (containing the embryo) and the ovary wall develops into the FRUIT, which protects the seeds and aids their dispersal.

The male gamete (sperm) is small and motile, carrying paternal DNA to the egg. The female gamete (egg/ovum) is large and non-motile, carrying maternal DNA plus cytoplasm and nutrients for the early embryo.
Follicle-Stimulating Hormone from the pituitary stimulates the growth of a follicle in the ovary, which then secretes oestrogen. Rising oestrogen later inhibits FSH by negative feedback.
Luteinising Hormone from the pituitary. A mid-cycle SURGE in LH, triggered by high oestrogen (positive feedback), causes OVULATION and the formation of the corpus luteum.
Secreted by the growing follicle. It repairs and thickens the uterus lining. At low levels it inhibits FSH (negative feedback); at a high peak it triggers the LH surge (positive feedback).

Secreted by the corpus luteum after ovulation. It maintains the thickened uterus lining and inhibits FSH and LH (negative feedback). If no pregnancy, it falls, the lining is shed (menstruation) and the cycle restarts.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: variation needs BOTH meiosis and fertilisation
flashcards.back: Sexual variation comes from meiosis (making unique gametes) AND fertilisation (randomly combining them). Naming only one is an incomplete answer and loses a mark.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: 'compare' means comparisons, not two lists
flashcards.back: A compare-and-contrast question wants linked points (sexual = variation whereas asexual = clones). Two separate paragraphs that never connect score far fewer marks than the same facts written as comparisons.

quickCheck.prompt: Genetic variation among offspring produced by sexual reproduction arises from:
options: Mitosis only
Meiosis producing unique gametes AND the random fusion of gametes at fertilisation
Fertilisation only
The environment the offspring grow up in
quickCheck.answer: Meiosis producing unique gametes AND the random fusion of gametes at fertilisation

quickCheck.prompt: Offspring produced by asexual reproduction are best described as:
options: Genetically identical clones of the single parent
A random mixture of two parents' alleles
Always haploid
More variable than sexually produced offspring
quickCheck.answer: Genetically identical clones of the single parent

quickCheck.prompt: Why must meiosis occur before fertilisation in a sexual life cycle?
options: To increase the chromosome number so the zygote is polyploid
To halve the chromosome number so gametes are haploid, keeping the diploid number constant after fusion
To copy the parent exactly
Because fertilisation cannot occur in diploid cells
quickCheck.answer: To halve the chromosome number so gametes are haploid, keeping the diploid number constant after fusion

quickCheck.prompt: In a human of chromosome number 2n = 46, the chromosome number of a gamete and of the zygote formed at fertilisation are, respectively:
options: 46 and 46
23 and 23
23 and 46
46 and 92

quickCheck.prompt: The mid-cycle surge of LH that triggers ovulation is caused by:
options: A high level of oestrogen acting by positive feedback
Progesterone from the corpus luteum
A fall in FSH
The shedding of the uterus lining
quickCheck.answer: A high level of oestrogen acting by positive feedback

faq.q: If asexual reproduction is fast and needs no mate, why do so many organisms bother with sex at all?
faq.a: Because the currency that matters over the long run is genetic variation. Asexual reproduction copies one genome perfectly, which is superb when conditions are stable — but a clonal population is only as safe as that single genotype. One new disease or a shift in climate can wipe out every identical individual at once. Sexual reproduction constantly reshuffles alleles through meiosis and fertilisation, so a population contains many different genotypes. When the environment changes, some of those variants are likely to survive, and natural selection has raw material to act on. The trade-off is real: sex is slower, costs energy finding a mate, and only passes on half of each parent's genome. Many organisms hedge their bets by doing both — reproducing asexually when things are good and switching to sexual reproduction when conditions become stressful.

faq.q: What exactly is the difference between pollination and fertilisation in a flowering plant?
faq.a: They are two separate events and examiners often catch students using them interchangeably. Pollination is simply the physical TRANSFER of pollen from an anther to a stigma, carried by wind or an animal. Nothing has fused yet. Fertilisation happens afterwards: a pollen tube grows down through the style, and the male gamete inside the pollen fuses with the female gamete (the egg cell) in the ovule to form a diploid zygote. So pollination delivers the male gamete to the right place, and fertilisation is the actual fusion of gametes. After fertilisation the ovule becomes the seed and the ovary wall becomes the fruit.

faq.q: Why is the female gamete so much larger than the male gamete?
faq.a: Their roles are different. The male gamete's job is delivery — it only has to carry paternal DNA to the egg, so it is small, and in animals motile, and produced in huge numbers to maximise the chance one reaches the target. The female gamete has to do more than carry maternal DNA: it also stockpiles the cytoplasm, organelles and nutrients that the zygote will live on during its first divisions, before it can obtain resources any other way. That provisioning role is why the egg is large and non-motile. The same logic explains why plants make abundant tiny pollen but far fewer, resource-rich ovules.

faq.q: How can oestrogen both inhibit and stimulate the pituitary in the same cycle?
faq.a: This is the subtle part of menstrual-cycle control, and it is why the cycle needs both negative and positive feedback. For most of the follicular phase oestrogen is at a moderate level, and at that level it inhibits FSH release — classic negative feedback that stops too many follicles maturing. But as the dominant follicle grows, oestrogen climbs to a high peak, and above a threshold its effect flips: high oestrogen now stimulates a sharp surge of LH — positive feedback — which triggers ovulation. So the same hormone gives opposite signals depending on its concentration. After ovulation the corpus luteum's progesterone takes over the inhibition of FSH and LH, preventing further ovulation until it either is maintained by pregnancy or breaks down and the cycle restarts.

sections.content: The obvious question about reproduction is how offspring are made. The question D3.1 really cares about is sharper: what kind of genetic variation does each mode of reproduction produce, and why does that matter. Asexual reproduction copies a single parent to make identical clones; sexual reproduction combines two parents through gametes to make offspring that are all genetically different. Around that central contrast the topic hangs the machinery — meiosis making haploid gametes, fertilisation restoring the diploid number, reproduction in flowering plants, and the hormonal cycle that governs human reproduction. This lesson works through each part, and every worked example is set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, because in a compare-and-explain topic like this, HOW you phrase a point decides whether it scores.
Two modes of reproduction, two consequences for variation

Asexual reproduction involves a single parent and no fusion of gametes. The offspring are produced by mitosis and are therefore genetically identical to the parent and to each other — they are clones. It is fast, energetically cheap and needs no mate, which makes it a powerful strategy when an organism is already well suited to a stable environment. Its weakness is the flip side of its strength: because every individual shares one genotype, the population carries almost no genetic variation, so a single new threat can affect all of them equally. Sexual reproduction, by contrast, involves the fusion of two haploid gametes, usually from two parents. It generates genetic variation, so offspring differ from one another and from their parents. That variation is the raw material for natural selection and lets a population adapt when conditions change. The cost is that it is slower and requires finding a mate.

items: **Asexual:** one parent, offspring are genetically identical clones (via mitosis); fast, no mate needed; little variation.
**Sexual:** two parents, gametes fuse; offspring genetically different; slower and needs a mate; generates variation.
**Key consequence:** the amount of genetic variation is the fundamental difference — none from asexual (barring mutation), plenty from sexual.
**Why it matters:** variation gives adaptability to a changing environment; identical clones are vulnerable to a single new threat such as disease.

sections.content: Gametes, meiosis and fertilisation

A gamete is a specialised sex cell that carries just one set of chromosomes — it is haploid (n). Gametes are produced by meiosis, a form of cell division that halves the chromosome number. This halving is essential: at fertilisation two gametes fuse to form a zygote, and if the gametes were diploid the chromosome number would double every generation. Instead, meiosis (2n to n) followed by fertilisation (n to 2n) keeps the chromosome number constant from one generation to the next. In a human, for example, a body cell has 46 chromosomes (2n = 46), each gamete has 23 (n = 23), and the zygote formed when sperm meets egg has 46 again.

Fertilisation does more than restore the diploid number — it is one of the two engines of genetic variation in sexual reproduction. Meiosis is the first engine: through independent assortment of chromosomes and crossing over, every gamete produced carries a different combination of alleles, so no two gametes are genetically identical. Fertilisation is the second engine: which particular sperm fuses with which particular egg is a matter of chance, so a huge number of genetically distinct zygotes is possible from the same two parents. It is the combination of meiosis AND fertilisation that makes each sexually produced offspring genetically unique — naming only one of the two is an incomplete explanation.

items: **Gametes are haploid (n)** — produced by meiosis, which halves the chromosome number.
**Fertilisation restores diploid (2n)** — fusion of two gametes gives a zygote with the full chromosome number.
**Meiosis + fertilisation keeps 2n constant** across generations; without meiosis the number would double each time.
**Variation comes from BOTH** — meiosis makes each gamete unique (independent assortment, crossing over) and fertilisation randomly combines them.

sections.question: In an organism with a diploid number of 2n = 8, state the number of chromosomes in (a) a gamete and (b) a zygote formed at fertilisation, and (c) explain why meiosis must occur at some point in the life cycle. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a)** A gamete is haploid, so it has $n = 4$ chromosomes. [1 mark]

**(b)** At fertilisation two gametes fuse, restoring the diploid number, so the zygote has $2n = 8$ chromosomes. [1 mark]

**(c)** Meiosis halves the chromosome number to produce haploid gametes. [1 mark] Without meiosis, fertilisation would double the chromosome number every generation; meiosis (2n to n) followed by fertilisation (n to 2n) keeps the diploid number constant. [1 mark]

*Each of the four sentences is a distinct creditable point — the engine credits them independently, so a correct part (b) still scores even if part (a) were wrong (error-carried-forward on the reasoning).*

sections.content: Reproduction in flowering plants (overview)

Flowering plants reproduce sexually, and their life cycle involves the same haploid-gamete, diploid-zygote logic, packaged differently. The male gametes are carried inside pollen grains made in the anthers; the female gametes (egg cells) sit inside ovules within the ovary. Two distinct events move the process forward. First is pollination — the transfer of pollen from an anther to a stigma, carried by wind or by animals such as insects. Pollination simply delivers the male gamete to the right place; nothing has fused yet. Second is fertilisation: after landing on a compatible stigma, the pollen grain grows a pollen tube down through the style to an ovule, and the male gamete fuses with the female gamete to form a diploid zygote. Keeping these two events separate is a common exam requirement.

After fertilisation the flower is transformed. The fertilised ovule develops into a seed, which contains the embryo plant and a food store. The wall of the ovary develops into the fruit, which encloses and protects the seeds and often aids their dispersal — a fleshy fruit tempts an animal to eat it and carry the seeds away, while a dry winged fruit catches the wind. Because two parents contribute gametes, the seeds are genetically variable, exactly as in animal sexual reproduction.

items: **Pollination:** transfer of pollen (male gamete) from anther to stigma, by wind or animals — delivery, not fusion.
**Fertilisation:** pollen tube grows down the style; male gamete fuses with the egg cell in the ovule to form a diploid zygote.
**Seed:** the fertilised ovule develops into a seed containing the embryo and a food store.
**Fruit:** the ovary wall develops into the fruit, which protects the seeds and aids dispersal.

sections.content: Reproduction in humans: the gametes

Human reproduction is sexual, so it depends on two very different gametes whose forms match their jobs. The male gamete, the sperm, is small and motile, with a flagellum for swimming and little more than a nucleus, mitochondria and an enzyme-filled acrosome. Its role is delivery: to carry paternal DNA to the egg, and sperm are produced in enormous numbers to raise the chance that one reaches its target. The female gamete, the egg (ovum), is large and non-motile. As well as carrying maternal DNA, it stockpiles cytoplasm, organelles and nutrients that will sustain the zygote through its first divisions before it can obtain resources any other way. When a sperm fertilises the egg, the two haploid nuclei fuse, restoring the diploid number and combining alleles from both parents.
Hormonal control of the menstrual cycle

In humans the release of the female gamete is governed by a roughly 28-day menstrual cycle controlled by four hormones: FSH and LH from the pituitary gland, and oestrogen and progesterone from the ovary. The cycle is a beautiful example of feedback control, using both negative and positive feedback. It begins as FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone) stimulates a follicle in the ovary to grow. The growing follicle secretes oestrogen, which repairs and thickens the lining of the uterus in preparation for a possible pregnancy. At moderate levels this rising oestrogen inhibits further FSH release — negative feedback that stops too many follicles maturing at once.

As the dominant follicle matures, oestrogen climbs to a high peak, and above a threshold its effect reverses: high oestrogen now stimulates a sharp surge of LH (Luteinising Hormone) — this is positive feedback. The LH surge, around day 14, triggers ovulation: the release of the egg from the follicle. The empty follicle then becomes the corpus luteum, which secretes progesterone (and some oestrogen). Progesterone maintains the thickened uterus lining, keeping it ready for implantation, and it inhibits FSH and LH by negative feedback, which prevents any further ovulation. If the egg is not fertilised, the corpus luteum breaks down after about two weeks, progesterone and oestrogen levels fall, the uterus lining is shed (menstruation), and the fall in these hormones releases FSH from inhibition so that a new cycle begins.

items: **FSH:** stimulates a follicle to grow, which then secretes oestrogen.
**Oestrogen:** thickens the uterus lining; at low levels inhibits FSH (negative feedback), at a high peak triggers the LH surge (positive feedback).
**LH:** its mid-cycle surge triggers OVULATION and forms the corpus luteum.
**Progesterone:** from the corpus luteum, maintains the uterus lining and inhibits FSH and LH (negative feedback); its fall causes menstruation and restarts the cycle.
**Both feedback types feature:** negative feedback dominates and stabilises the cycle; one burst of positive feedback (high oestrogen to LH surge) drives ovulation.

sections.content: The single most common menstrual-cycle error is mixing up which hormone does what. Anchor each one to its source and its job: FSH grows the follicle; oestrogen (from the follicle) thickens the lining and, at its peak, flips to trigger the LH surge; LH causes ovulation; progesterone (from the corpus luteum) maintains the lining. If you can also state whether each feedback step is negative or positive, you will pick up the reasoning marks that weaker answers miss.

sections.question: A blood test on day 22 of a regular 28-day menstrual cycle shows a high level of progesterone and low levels of FSH and LH, and no pregnancy has occurred. Explain these hormone levels and predict what will happen over the next week. [4]
sections.solution:
1. Day 22 is in the second half of the cycle, after ovulation, when the corpus luteum has formed from the follicle. [1 mark]
2. The corpus luteum secretes progesterone, which is why progesterone is high; it maintains the thickened uterus lining ready for implantation. [1 mark]
3. Progesterone (with oestrogen) inhibits the pituitary from releasing FSH and LH — negative feedback — which is why FSH and LH are low, and this prevents any further ovulation. [1 mark]
4. Because there is no pregnancy, the corpus luteum will break down over the next week; progesterone will fall, so the uterus lining is shed (menstruation), and the fall in progesterone releases FSH, starting a new cycle. [1 mark]

*Four distinct, sequential points, each worth one mark. The engine credits them independently and applies error-carried-forward, so a correct prediction in point 4 still scores even if an earlier line were phrased imperfectly.*

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Naming only one source of sexual variation** — variation needs BOTH meiosis (making unique gametes) AND fertilisation (randomly combining them). Writing only 'meiosis' or only 'fertilisation' is incomplete.
**Calling asexual offspring 'similar' rather than identical** — asexually produced offspring are genetically IDENTICAL clones (barring mutation); 'similar' or 'slightly different' loses the mark.

**Writing two separate lists instead of comparisons** — a compare-and-contrast question wants linked points (sexual gives variation WHEREAS asexual gives clones), not one paragraph on each with no connection.
**Confusing pollination with fertilisation** — pollination is transfer of pollen to the stigma; fertilisation is the later fusion of gametes in the ovule. They are different events.
**Forgetting that gametes are haploid and the zygote diploid** — meiosis makes haploid (n) gametes; fertilisation restores diploid (2n). Saying gametes are diploid is a serious error.

**Muddling the menstrual-cycle hormones** — attributing ovulation to FSH, or the LH surge to progesterone, is a classic slip. LH surge causes ovulation; the surge itself is triggered by high oestrogen (positive feedback).
**Saying oestrogen only inhibits the pituitary** — it inhibits FSH at moderate levels (negative feedback) but triggers the LH surge at its peak (positive feedback). Both effects must be recognised.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Compare-and-contrast questions in D3.1 are marked analytically: each distinct valid comparison is worth one mark. The engine is looking for linked comparative points — a statement about sexual reproduction set against the matching statement about asexual reproduction — not two disconnected lists. Answer marks (A) credit a correct comparative point, method reasoning credits the underlying idea, and error-carried-forward means a slip early on does not sink the points that follow. Study how each mark below attaches to a specific, named comparison rather than to loose phrasing.

sections.question: Compare and contrast sexual and asexual reproduction in terms of genetic variation and its advantages. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Sexual reproduction involves two parents whose haploid gametes fuse at fertilisation, so the offspring are genetically DIFFERENT from one another and from their parents (genetic variation), whereas asexual reproduction involves a single parent and produces offspring that are genetically IDENTICAL clones. The variation from sexual reproduction gives the population adaptability, so that some offspring are likely to survive if the environment changes, whereas the advantage of asexual reproduction is that it is fast and needs no mate, making it efficient when the environment is stable. Both processes produce new individuals of the species, but sexual reproduction supplies the variation that natural selection acts on, whereas asexual reproduction preserves a successful genotype unchanged.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid COMPARISON scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Comparison 1 (A):** sexual = two parents / gametes fuse at fertilisation, WHEREAS asexual = one parent / no gamete fusion.
- **Comparison 2 (A):** sexual offspring are genetically DIFFERENT (variation), WHEREAS asexual offspring are genetically IDENTICAL clones.
- **Comparison 3 (A):** advantage of sexual = variation gives adaptability to a changing environment, WHEREAS advantage of asexual = fast / no mate needed / efficient in a stable environment.
- **Comparison 4 (A):** a further valid comparative point — e.g. sexual supplies raw material for natural selection WHEREAS asexual preserves a proven genotype; or sexual is slower / costly WHEREAS asexual is rapid.

Because the marks are for DISTINCT comparisons, the engine credits a point only once — restating 'sexual gives variation' in two ways scores a single mark. Equivalent wording is accepted (for example 'offspring vary' for 'genetically different', or 'copies of the parent' for 'clones'), and error-carried-forward means a valid advantage still scores even if the candidate mislabels a chromosome number elsewhere.

**What the engine rejects.** Two separate lists that never link the two modes score poorly: an answer that describes sexual reproduction in one paragraph and asexual reproduction in another, with no comparative connection, is credited only where a genuine contrast can be read across them. The command term is 'compare and contrast', so the engine rewards the WHEREAS-style linkage, not parallel description.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, give four distinct comparative points, each pairing a feature of sexual reproduction against the matching feature of asexual reproduction, and be sure to cover both the variation itself and its advantage.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — comparison by comparison, with equivalents accepted and error-carried-forward, capped at the marks available. So write in linked contrasts: for every claim about sexual reproduction, add the matching claim about asexual reproduction in the same sentence (using 'whereas' or 'in contrast'). Two tidy lists feel complete but score as description; the engine rewards genuine comparisons, and covering both the variation and its advantage is what protects your marks.
Where this leads

The central idea of D3.1 — that sexual reproduction manufactures genetic variation while asexual reproduction preserves a genotype — is the hinge that connects the whole of biology's continuity-and-change theme. Meiosis and fertilisation reappear in inheritance and in the mechanisms of evolution; the variation they generate is precisely what natural selection sorts. Master the habit of asking 'what does this mode of reproduction do to variation, and what does that variation buy the population', and you have the reasoning template that unlocks genetics, evolution and biodiversity alike.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 answer marked: compare sexual and asexual reproduction for genetic variation, or explain the hormonal control of the menstrual cycle
